summary:
Augustine of Hippo (354–430 AD) was the most influential theologian in the history of Western Christianity — a North African intellectual who converted from [[event:2080|Manichaeism]] through Neoplatonism to Christianity under the influence of Bishop Ambrose of Milan, and whose theological writings shaped Catholic, Protestant, and broader Western thought so profoundly that one could argue Western civilization itself (including its secular variants) bears his intellectual imprint. Born in Thagaste (modern Algeria) to a pagan father and Christian mother (Monica, later canonized), Augustine had a long spiritual journey — his autobiography the Confessions, written c. 397 AD, is the first great work of introspective self-examination in Western literature and remains one of the most widely read books of antiquity.

As Bishop of Hippo Regius (from 395 AD until his death as the [[event:2037|Vandals]] were besieging the city), Augustine produced a vast body of work on grace, free will, predestination, original sin, the sacraments, and political theology. His 'City of God' (413–426 AD), written partly in response to pagan claims that Christianity caused Rome's fall, articulated a theology of history contrasting the City of God (the community of the saved) with the Earthly City (secular political power) — a framework that shaped Christian political thought for centuries. His doctrine of original sin (that Adam's fall corrupted human nature, requiring divine grace for salvation) and his theology of predestination became foundational for Catholicism and were later radicalized by Calvin into Calvinist predestination. Augustine is a Doctor of the Catholic Church and his influence pervades medieval scholasticism, the Reformation, and modern theology.
